reason: RQ1: extends — difficulty-aware dynamic workflow generation selects multi-agent configurations per task, relevant to adaptive task dispatch in decision agent teams (WWW2026).

RQ1: extends — Orchestrated Distributed Intelligence reconceptualizes multi-agent AI as cohesive orchestrated networks with multi-loop feedback, a potential paradigm-level reframe of decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — state-aware routing encodes interaction history and agent knowledge to select the most suitable worker at each step, directly addressing worker selection in decision agent teams.
RQ3: extends — evaluates plan adherence fidelity in agentic systems, directly relevant to whether decision agent can independently close the loop without long-term assistant context.
RQ1: extends — hierarchical agent trees decompose complex goals into subgoals with control flow, an alternative architectural pattern for multi-agent workers team task decomposition.
RQ2: extends — cross-team orchestration mechanism addresses how a manager-level scheduler (openclaw) coordinates multiple decision agent worker teams.
RQ2: extends — context engineering for corporate multi-agent architecture is directly relevant to how openclaw maintains and passes long-horizon context as manager above decision agent.
RQ1: extends — unified orchestration layer integrating MCP and A2A protocols addresses coordination infrastructure for multi-agent decision systems.
RQ1: extends — taxonomy of agentic AI architectures covering the transition from single-agent loops to organized multi-agent systems provides landscape context for decision agent positioning.
RQ1: extends — comprehensive survey of multi-agent collaboration mechanisms covering actor types, structures, strategies, and coordination protocols provides foundational landscape.
RQ1: extends — 2024 general survey on LLM multi-agents, superseded by more recent and focused surveys; no methodological contribution on decision agent architecture.
RQ1: extends — general LLM agent methodology survey with broad scope, tangential to the specific multi-agent workers team architecture of the decision agent.
RQ1: orthogonal — domain-specific application of multi-agent orchestration to IT incident response; architectural insights not transferable to general decision agent design.
RQ1: orthogonal — multi-agent framework narrowly scoped to agile software engineering tasks within a single domain, not general decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — focuses on user personalization and preference learning for single-agent systems, not multi-agent team coordination or decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — survey focuses on RL training methods for individual LLMs, not on deployment-time multi-agent decision architectures or team coordination.
no RQ: orthogonal — evaluation methodology survey for LLM agents, tangential to architectural questions in RQ1–3 about team coordination and standalone closure.
RQ1: extends — general agentic LLM survey without focus on multi-agent team coordination; superseded by more targeted sources already in the skim list.
no RQ: orthogonal — practitioner best-practices guide for production agentic workflows, not a research contribution to multi-agent decision architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — meta-tool optimization addresses redundant tool-call patterns in single-agent workflows, not multi-agent team coordination or decision architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — formal specification language for agent resource constraints; not directly addressing multi-agent team coordination or decision architecture.
no RQ: reject — agentic RAG focuses on retrieval-augmented generation pipelines; no relevance to multi-agent team coordination or decision agent architecture.
no RQ: orthogonal — focuses on RL training methods for multi-agent coordination games, not on LLM-based decision agent architectural design for end-to-end task completion.
RQ1: extends — cooperative behavior profiles predict multi-agent team performance, informing agent selection and composition for decision agent worker teams.
RQ1: extends — role-based author-reviewer-metareviewer hierarchy achieves 50% efficiency gain over multi-agent debate, relevant to role design in decision agent worker teams.
RQ1: extends — systematic analysis of 94 papers identifying role-based cooperation as dominant MAS design pattern, providing actionable design guidance for decision agent worker architecture.
RQ1: extends — IEEE-published survey of agent workflow architectures including multi-agent collaboration patterns and orchestration strategies, contextualizing decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — autonomous multi-agent optimization via iterative refinement feedback loops (Refinement, Execution, Evaluation agents), modeling a self-improving decision agent worker team.
RQ1: extends — structured taxonomy of multi-agent communication dimensions (who, what, when, where, why) informs protocol design for decision agent worker team coordination.
RQ1: extends — foundational two-stage team optimization and task-solving framework (DyLAN), provides historical baseline for dynamic decision agent worker team assembly.
RQ1: extends — enumerates open challenges in LLM multi-agent systems including collective reasoning and safety, framing the research gaps decision agent must address.
RQ3: orthogonal — reframes agent evaluation around financial ROI rather than capability; tangentially relevant to standalone decision agent deployment viability and adoption.
no RQ: orthogonal — interpretability methods for agentic systems; tangential to RQ1 via understanding agent decision processes, but no direct architecture or collaboration contribution.
no RQ: reject — applies MAS to social science simulation; no decision agent architecture or worker team methodology applicable to the thesis.
no RQ: reject — data systems and serving infrastructure perspective on agentic workflows; no contribution to decision agent design, collaboration, or standalone operation.
no RQ: reject — formal verification of agent safety and security properties; skim budget exhausted by higher-relevance candidates; no architecture or collaboration methodology.

RQ1: extends — co-evolving decision agent + learnable skill bank achieves autonomous long-horizon task completion, directly modeling multi-agent worker architecture with self-improving skill discovery.
RQ2: extends — write-manage-read memory taxonomy covering personal assistants and collaborative teams; foundational to how openclaw provides persistent context feeding the decision agent.

RQ1: extends — MCTS-based dynamic orchestration with dual-memory (working + experience) as end-to-end task completion mechanism; novel orchestration pattern though domain-specific to clinical reasoning.

RQ1/RQ3: extends — DAG-based execution harness (SGH) with immutable plans and separated planning/recovery layers; position paper identifying structural weaknesses in agent loops relevant to decision agent reliability.
RQ1: extends — maps LLM-agent architectures to classical planning methodologies; web-specific but planning taxonomy applicable to decision agent task decomposition.
RQ1: extends — studies emergent coordination behavior in multi-agent LLM systems without explicit orchestration design; foundational for understanding organic dynamics in workers-team.
RQ1: extends — LLM high-level planning with MARL for multi-agent coordination; tangentially relevant to how decision agent orchestrates workers via learned policies.

RQ1: extends — non-parametric cross-episode decision evolution via natural-language playbook; mechanism applicable to decision agent skill adaptation but domain-specific to spacecraft (CVPR 2026 AI4Space Workshop).
no RQ direct match: orthogonal — Bayesian belief aggregation across LLMs for domain-specific cost-aware decisions; shares multi-LLM orchestration vocabulary but not workers-team architecture design.
no RQ direct match: orthogonal — evaluation/governance synthesis framework identifying a governance-to-action closure gap; no architectural contribution to decision agent design.
RQ1: extends — 2024 survey of agentic workflow components and LLM profiling; foundational context but predates current landscape coverage and adds no new contribution.
no RQ: reject — pure serving/inference infrastructure library separating workflow policy from execution; no contribution to decision agent coordination architecture or task completion methodology.
no RQ: reject — agentic workflow serving and batching optimization; no architectural contribution to decision agent design or multi-agent coordination.

RQ1: extends — graph-based agent selection (node sampling) and bidirectional message passing as coordination topology for multi-agent workers team; ICLR 2026 accepted, strong lab pedigree (James Zou, Guohao Li, Tianlong Chen).

RQ1: extends — systematic survey of LLM agent optimization (RL, fine-tuning, prompt engineering) published in ACM Computing Surveys; incremental relative to existing RL coverage in the landscape but high-gravity reference.

RQ1: extends — advocates BDI architectures, communication protocols, and mechanism design as principled foundations for agentic AI; foundational position relevant to decision agent design but no empirical contribution.
RQ1: orthogonal — LLM-derived graph priors enhance MARL coordination in particle environments; cooperative MARL setting, not LLM-based decision agent workers team for end-to-end task completion.

RQ1: extends — hierarchical temporal logic for LLM-driven multi-agent task planning; robotics domain limits transferability but hierarchical task decomposition mechanism tangentially relevant to decision agent planning.

RQ1: orthogonal — empirical study of development patterns and coordination issues across 8 open-source multi-agent frameworks; practical engineering insights without architectural methodology contribution.
RQ2: orthogonal — uses uncertainty as a planning signal in multi-turn goal-oriented conversation; conversational AI domain, not multi-agent task execution or openclaw-style orchestration.
no RQ: orthogonal — conceptual taxonomy distinguishing AI agents from agentic AI; definitional framing with no methodological contribution to decision agent or workers team design.
no RQ: reject — analyzes agentic AI execution from a CPU and hardware performance perspective; no agent architecture or decision-making methodology relevant to any RQ.

no RQ: reject — organizational guide for adopting agentic AI addressing change management and human orchestration roles; no technical methodology for decision agent architecture or workers team design.
no RQ: reject — multi-robot physical collaboration via task-level similarity memoization; robotics execution domain with no overlap to LLM-based decision agent workers team architecture.

RQ1,RQ3: extends — identifies step-wise greedy reasoning as fundamental planning failure for long-horizon tasks; FLARE's future-aware lookahead directly addresses how decision agents achieve end-to-end task closure and standalone operation.

RQ1: extends — scaling law for orchestration topology and dynamic pattern routing (parallel/sequential/hierarchical/hybrid) addresses Dolphin's static vs. adaptive scheduling question; 12-23% improvement over static baselines; single-author preprint, gravity low.

RQ1: extends — CAMCO runtime constraint projection engine for policy-compliant enterprise multi-agent orchestration adds safety enforcement layer complementing ISF security layer; zero violations, 92-97% utility retention; practitioner paper (IBM/Salesforce), gravity low.

RQ1: extends — empirical comparison of subagent (parallel+consolidation) vs agent-team (specialist+handoff) modes provides concrete data on when worker team coordination outperforms parallel aggregation; incremental relative to existing multi-agent comparisons in landscape.

RQ1: extends — survey of multi-tool orchestration covering cross-tool dependency modeling, sequential and parallel scheduling, and failure recovery; relevant to ContextLoader's multi-step tool call management but scope is survey-level, novelty incremental.

RQ1: extends — enterprise stateful benchmark requiring agents to maintain coherent state across long interleaved tool-call sequences with access-control policies; directly tests the Decision Agent multi-step AutoFlow scenario.

RQ1: supports — design and evaluation framework for enterprise GenAI multi-agent collaboration showing up to 70% goal-success improvement over single agent; empirical grounding for thesis multi-agent priority; Dec 2024 preprint, novelty incremental relative to newer 2026 landscape.

RQ1: extends — long-horizon planning improvement for LLM agents; incremental relative to FLARE [3] already in notes which covers the same greedy-vs-lookahead gap with stricter formalization and stronger empirical results.
no RQ: reject — domain-specific application of planner-executor orchestration to materials science HPC; no methodological contribution applicable to enterprise decision agent architecture.

RQ1: challenges — systematic empirical result that homogeneous multi-agent workflow is matched by optimized single agent with KV cache reuse (OneFlow), directly questions whether multi-agent workers team coordination is the core architectural bottleneck claimed in thesis.

RQ1: extends — dynamic tool retrieval conditioned on evolving multi-step execution plan (23–104% success rate gain over static retrievers) addresses multi-step tool dependency chaining; incremental relative to 2603.20313 but adds execution-context conditioning angle relevant to Decision Agent AutoFlow sequences.

RQ1: extends — intent-scoring + state-aware lazy schema loading on 120-tool benchmark (95% per-turn token reduction) validates ContextLoader direction; caution: end-to-end performance figures are projections from token measurements, not live LLM agent measurements.

RQ1: extends — three-layer ontological grounding (Role/Domain/Interaction ontologies) in enterprise agentic systems parallels BKN's semantic decoupling architecture; cross-industry empirical validation (FinTech, Insurance, Healthcare) provides comparator for BKN's domain-grounding approach.

RQ1: extends — expert-rubric evaluation framework (LH-Bench) for long-horizon subjective enterprise tasks provides measurement methodology applicable to Decision Agent's multi-step AutoFlow quality assessment; kappa 0.60 domain-expert vs 0.46 LLM-generated rubrics informs evaluation design.

RQ3: orthogonal — stateless agent learning across hundreds of open-ended game episodes; no enterprise multi-step workflow, tool selection, or coordination mechanism; experience-to-policy conversion focus does not address worker independence in Decision Agent context.

no RQ: reject — environmental science domain application of multi-agent workflows; no decision agent architecture, tool management, or coordination mechanism contribution applicable to enterprise task context.

RQ1: extends — five-axis auditability framework (action recoverability / lifecycle coverage / policy checkability / responsibility attribution / evidence integrity) plus 8.3ms-overhead pre-execution mediation directly maps to Decision Agent's 全链路可审计 requirement and Dolphin observability layer.

RQ1: extends — training-free latent-space inter-agent communication claims lossless info transfer with 70.8–83.7% token reduction, addresses 协同失真 mechanism but is in tension with Decision Agent's auditability anti-pattern (latent traffic is non-traceable); skim to extract fidelity metric, not mechanism.

RQ1: extends — LongHorizon-Bench with FRP/RCS/CRR/CAR axes for regulated decisioning (loan / insurance) directly matches the enterprise long-chain framing of Decision Agent; novel Compliance Reconstruction axis is candidate input for ISF-layer audit metrics.

RQ1: extends — shared vector space embedding tools+parent agents with metadata links yields Recall@5 +19.4% on LiveMCPBench; useful baseline against the graph approach in 2511.18194 but mechanism is vector-only and overlaps the deep-read's evaluation set.

RQ1: extends — two-level dense-embedding retrieval hierarchy claims scaling to thousands of MCP tools across dozens of agents; provides a precision/coverage benchmarking point for ContextLoader but pure-vector approach is a less-structured baseline to BKN.

RQ1: extends — categorizes tool hallucinations into selection vs usage and proposes reliability alignment training; complements the BKN structural defense for tool hallucination but uses alignment training rather than semantic decoupling.
RQ1: orthogonal — reliable agent trajectories in IR domain shares 'Plan-Reason-Act trajectory observability' vocabulary but the IR-specific framing limits transfer; one-paragraph note only.
RQ1: orthogonal — large real-world MCP benchmark useful as evaluation harness reference for ContextLoader, but is benchmark-only with limited mechanism contribution per anti-patterns.

no RQ: reject — robotic manipulation with VLA in physical state space; deterministic embodiment assumptions do not transfer to enterprise non-deterministic data tasks (matches anti-pattern: 仅在游戏/形式化状态空间环境验证).
no RQ: reject — code translation pipeline is a narrow application domain; no contribution to coordination, planning, or tool retrieval mechanisms relevant to RQ1–RQ3.

no RQ: reject — vision-domain latent communication; LatentMAS (skimmed) already covers the latent-channel mechanism with broader text-task evidence, this is duplicative for Decision Agent's text-tool setting.
no RQ: reject — survey paper without new mechanism; matches anti-pattern '纯基准测评没有机制分析' (extended to surveys); mine references later if needed.
no RQ: reject — multi-robot embodied task planning; physical-world assumptions and prompt-only optimization do not transfer to enterprise context.
no RQ: reject — graph-library specific function calling for graph-math problems; narrow tool domain that does not generalize to general enterprise tool catalogs.

RQ1: extends — knowledge-graph node/edge representation with weighted reciprocal rank fusion for retrieving tools and parent agents at the scale of thousands of MCPs directly parallels BKN semantic structuring of ContextLoader, with Recall@5 +14.9% / nDCG@5 +14.6% on LiveMCPBench.

RQ1: extends — empirical horizon-degradation diagnostic across 3,100+ trajectories on GPT-5/Claude-4 over 4 domains; complements FLARE (note 3) with FMEA-guided structured analysis rather than theoretical greedy-policy proof.
RQ1: extends — formal reliability metrics (RDC, VAF, GDS, MOP) for long-horizon agents; useful framework for quantifying Dolphin long-chain degradation but not yet a mechanism.

RQ1: extends — context-pollution problem in concurrent multi-agent orchestrators with two-mode focus sessions; relevant to Dolphin orchestrator/worker context isolation but conceptually adjacent rather than a core mechanism.
RQ1: extends — RL-trained context curation maximizing real-time information density; alternative to ContextLoader's heuristic dynamic loading but training-time approach.

RQ1: supports — vector-retrieval ReAct that loads only query-relevant tools achieving constant memory regardless of registry size; supports ContextLoader's design premise but lighter-weight than DTDR.
RQ1: extends — single-forward-pass hidden-activation detector for tool-call hallucinations (86.4% accuracy); orthogonal to BKN's structural defense but offers a runtime-cheap monitoring layer.
RQ1: extends — attention-topology spectral method for detecting syntactically-valid-but-semantically-wrong tool calls; complementary detection layer to BKN structural decoupling.
RQ1: orthogonal — runtime memory store for oversized tool outputs; engineering trick rather than mechanism analysis, but tangentially relevant to ContextLoader operational concerns.

RQ1: extends — neuro-symbolic dual-memory for long-horizon planning progress/feasibility tracking; potential mechanism for Dolphin long-chain state, but generic memory framework rather than coordination-specific.
no RQ: reject — adversarial attack on tool retrieval (semantic covering to hide valid tools); security threat model orthogonal to Decision Agent's mechanism-design RQs.
no RQ: reject — AI search-specific retrieval agent; narrow application domain that doesn't generalize to multi-tool enterprise decision flows.

RQ1: extends — MAST taxonomy of 14 multi-agent failure modes from 1,600+ annotated traces (κ=0.88) directly addresses Dolphin worker-team coordination gap; the landscape has no empirical MAS failure-mode taxonomy yet.

RQ1: supports — empirical degradation across multi-app office workflows (o3 72.83 → 30.36 single-app to three-app) supports thesis claim about long-chain planning degradation; benchmark-only contribution limits novelty for mechanism transfer.

RQ1: extends — ProtocolBench across A2A/ACP/ANP/Agora measures inter-protocol completion-time variance up to 36.5% and Fail-Storm recovery; relevant to FC2 inter-agent misalignment quantification (MAST) but at protocol-choice rather than mechanism layer.

RQ2: extends — CAT7 schema + SVAF field-level acceptance + lineage tracking targets cross-session inter-agent context retention, but single-author and abstract-only production claim with no benchmarks limits confidence; conceptually adjacent to openclaw-decision-agent context handoff.
RQ1: supports — quantifies long-horizon execution degradation; complements HORIZON (already in seen ledger) without adding distinct mechanism analysis.
RQ1: extends — training-time approach to extremely long-horizon agent tasks; potentially relevant to long-chain workers but training-time scope partially conflicts with deployment-time triage focus.
RQ2: orthogonal — position paper on inter-agent protocol standardization; minimal mechanism content, contextual reading only.
no RQ: orthogonal — cryptographic attestation and adversarial audit framing differs from Decision Agent's normal-workflow observability concern; single-author limits gravity.
RQ1: orthogonal — parallel tool calling for search efficiency; tangential to ContextLoader's selection mechanism, may inform Dolphin parallel-step scheduling but not core gap.
no RQ: reject — security/resource-amplification attack on tool calling chains; off-topic for Decision Agent architecture and tool-management research.
no RQ: reject — pure benchmark without methodological contribution per project exclusion criteria.
no RQ: reject — survey paper; no new mechanism, low marginal value over ProtocolBench (skim above).
RQ1: orthogonal — unification across function-calling protocols rather than retrieval/selection mechanism that ContextLoader needs.
RQ1: orthogonal — generic skill-architecture overview likely overlapping with COS-PLAY/Voyager already covered in landscape §1.1; no clear novel mechanism signal from search excerpt.

RQ1: extends — active tool requesting + hierarchical semantic routing over ~3,000 tools / 308 servers with 98% token reduction directly tests ContextLoader's 90%+ context-saving claim at scale beyond Agent-as-a-Graph's 527 tools, and proposes a paradigm (active/proactive) distinct from retrieval-then-rerank.

RQ3: extends — Episode/Note two-tier memory + Topic-Aware Event–Surprise dual-channel segmentation + Conflict-aware Memory Reconsolidation; alternate mechanism for goal 5 hierarchical memory; skim to compare with Collaborative Memory's permission-centric approach.

RQ1: extends — partitions procedural memory between orchestrator (task decomposition) and worker agents (execution); orchestrator-vs-worker memory split is design-relevant to Composer + Shared Workspace allocation question.

RQ1: extends — propagation graph + 3 failure modes (cascade amplification / topological sensitivity / consensus inertia) + genealogy-graph governance plugin (defense rate 0.32→0.89); complements MAST FC2 with mechanism-level cascade quantification, directly relevant to Shared Workspace error containment.
RQ2: extends — PROBE three-stage pipeline (search bottlenecks / identify / resolve), SOTA only 40%; a benchmark dimension for goal 2 proactive Runtime that complements Heartbeat mechanism paper.
RQ2: supports — early formalization of reactive→proactive paradigm shift; provides terminological grounding for goal 2 (Heartbeat) but predates 2026 Heartbeat-driven mechanism work.

RQ1: supports — 2800 tools / 308 servers with multi-persona queries; raises tool retrieval evaluation closer to enterprise 5k–10k target; but builds on MCP-Zero data and lacks new mechanism — skim for benchmark to validate BKN against.
RQ3: extends — graph-structured hierarchical memory; alternate path to goal 5; skim to compare graph vs episode/note structure choices.
RQ1: challenges — quantifies how group size amplifies collective misalignment; potentially constrains Composer team-size choices and FC2 scaling assumptions in Shared Workspace design.
RQ3: extends — role-aligned memory templates per agent; directly maps to Role tier of User/Role/Org hierarchy; skim for template-design patterns.
RQ3: extends — working/episodic/semantic memory separation with bounded context growth; skim for cross-session drift control mechanism applicable to Memory governance.
no RQ: reject — 'proactive' here means safety enforcement (probabilistic model checking), not Runtime initiative; orthogonal to goal 2 mechanism design.
no RQ: reject — survey only, no new mechanism; security framing not aligned with goal 5 governance/decay/audit core.
no RQ: reject — focuses on alignment-property influence on coordination outcomes, not Decision Agent's mechanism-level Shared Workspace question.
no RQ: reject — general-purpose hierarchical orchestration; competes with LangGraph/CrewAI category that thesis explicitly excludes (non-goal: 通用 DAG 工作流引擎).
no RQ: reject — generic divide-and-conquer multi-agent architecture; no governance / type-safe routing / enterprise context.
no RQ: reject — Captain Agent (2024) covers similar Composer-adjacent territory but predates 2026 work like 'From Skills to Talent' which we already deep-read; superseded for our purposes.
no RQ: reject — only 220 tools, smaller than HumanMCP (2800) already in skim; benchmark only, no new retrieval mechanism.
no RQ: reject — benchmark without new retrieval/dispatch mechanism; thesis Anti-pattern: '纯基准测评没有机制分析'.
no RQ: reject — generic LLM long-term memory benchmark; not agent-specific or hierarchical, off-target for goal 5 governance design.
no RQ: reject — proactivity here is open-world sensory triggering (wearables/IoT), not enterprise Runtime initiative; off-target for Decision Agent context.

RQ2: supports — periodic heartbeat scheduler dynamically dispatches Planner/Critic/Recaller/Dreamer cognitive modules; direct academic analog to Decision Agent 0.8 Heartbeat+Cron+Reflection design goal, which thesis flags as scarce in literature ('遇到必须收').
